metadata.title: Why Stars More Than 150 Times the Sun Cannot Exist
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = radiation pressure / Eddington limit / T^4 dominance; CRIMSON = gas pressure / stable stars / below Eddington

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: The most massive stars known hover around 150 solar masses. Stars much above this seem unable to hold themselves together. There appears to be a wall. Why?

card.copy: Why Stars More Than 150 Times the Sun Cannot Exist

beats.narration_text: Inside a star, two pressures balance gravity pulling inward: gas pressure from thermal motion of particles, and radiation pressure from photons pushing outward.

production_viz.label: Stellar pressure balance: gravity vs gas pressure + radiation pressure

beats.narration_text: Gas pressure scales as temperature. Radiation pressure scales as temperature to the fourth. In the most massive, hottest stars, which wins — and what happens when radiation pressure becomes too large?

card.copy: P_gas ∝ T. P_rad ∝ T^4. In the most massive stars, radiation pressure grows explosive. What is the consequence?

beats.narration_text: Gas pressure: proportional to T. Radiation pressure: proportional to T to the fourth. In cool stars, gas pressure dominates. Above about 40 solar masses, radiation pressure becomes comparable to gas pressure.

production_viz.label: P_gas ∝ T vs P_rad ∝ T^4: crossover near 40 M_sun

beats.narration_text: The Eddington luminosity is the critical luminosity where radiation pressure exactly balances gravity. It scales with mass: L sub Edd equals 4 pi G M c divided by kappa, where kappa is the opacity. Above L sub Edd, radiation blows the outer layers off.

production_viz.label: L_Edd = 4πGMc/κ: above this, radiation blows outer layers off

beats.narration_text: For the most massive stars, their intrinsic luminosity approaches or exceeds the Eddington limit. They shed mass continuously in strong stellar winds. Mass-loss is so high the star cannot grow indefinitely.

production_viz.label: Most massive stars: luminosity ≈ L_Edd, strong winds, mass shed continuously

beats.narration_text: This creates a natural mass ceiling. Stars that form above roughly 150 solar masses blow themselves apart — or peel away their outer layers — faster than they can accumulate mass. The Eddington limit enforces the ceiling.

production_viz.label: ~150 M_sun ceiling: above this, mass lost faster than gained

beats.narration_text: Very massive stars are also violently unstable — prone to giant eruptions called Luminous Blue Variable outbursts. Eta Carinae in the 1840s ejected several solar masses in a decade. The Eddington limit was being temporarily exceeded.

production_viz.label: Eta Carinae 1840s: LBV outburst, ejected several M_sun — Eddington exceeded

beats.narration_text: In the early universe, stars formed from pristine hydrogen and helium with no metals — lower opacity means higher Eddington limit. Population III stars may have reached thousands of solar masses. The ceiling is not universal.

production_viz.label: Pop III stars: no metals → lower opacity → higher Eddington ceiling

beats.narration_text: P gas proportional to T; P rad proportional to T to the fourth. Crossover near 40 solar masses. Eddington limit: L sub Edd proportional to M. Above about 150 solar masses: radiation pressure exceeds limit, mass blows off. Population III stars: lower opacity, higher ceiling.

card.copy: P_gas ∝ T, P_rad ∝ T^4. Crossover ~40 M_sun. L_Edd ∝ M. Above ~150 M_sun: mass blows off faster than gained. Pop III stars: no metals, higher Eddington ceiling.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
